statement: For a logistic-growth model of large-bodied terrestrial herbivores, r_max is the low-density intrinsic per-capita rate of increase. A LogNormal prior with log-scale mu=-1.609 and sigma=0.50 has median about 0.20 yr^-1 and places most mass on roughly 0.08-0.53 yr^-1, matching the slow-to-moderate maximum growth commonly used for ungulate populations.
source: Caughley, Analysis of Vertebrate Populations; Sinclair, Fryxell & Caughley, Wildlife Ecology, Conservation, and Management

statement: For a homogeneous temperate range supporting large grazing ungulates, K_density is the carrying capacity expressed as animals per square kilometer. A LogNormal prior with log-scale mu=2.303 and sigma=0.80 has median about 10 individuals km^-2 and is intentionally broad for habitat quality variation.
source: Caughley, Analysis of Vertebrate Populations; Damuth's mammalian population-density allometry

statement: For an animal population believed to be near a stationary equilibrium or carrying capacity, the realized one-year log growth g_t=log(N_{t+1}/N_t) is commonly centered near zero. A Normal prior or observation-scale model with mu=0 and sigma=0.20 yr^-1 allows typical annual multipliers of about exp(plus or minus 0.4) across a 95% range.
source: Morris & Doak, Quantitative Conservation Biology; Gotelli, A Primer of Ecology

statement: In stochastic population projections, sigma_env is the nonnegative standard deviation of annual environmental deviations in log population growth. A HalfNormal prior with sigma=0.25 yr^-1 is weakly informative for vertebrate monitoring data, favoring modest year-to-year variation while allowing larger environmental variability.

For long-lived large mammals in an age-structured population model, adult annual survival is usually high and less variable than recruitment. A Beta(45,5) prior has mean 0.90 and moderate concentration, representing the canonical high adult survival used in large-herbivore life-table analyses.
source: Caswell, Matrix Population Models; Gaillard et al., large-herbivore demography review

statement: In fish population dynamics, M is the instantaneous natural mortality rate excluding fishing. A Gamma prior with alpha=4 and beta=8 in shape-rate form has mean 0.50 yr^-1 and standard deviation 0.25 yr^-1, a conventional scale for many small-to-medium fish stocks when species-specific estimates are unavailable.
source: Beverton & Holt, On the Dynamics of Exploited Fish Populations; Pauly's empirical natural-mortality synthesis

statement: For a small aquatic predator-prey system fit with a Holling type-II functional response, a_attack is the search or attack coefficient when prey density is measured per liter and time in days. A LogNormal prior with log-scale mu=-2.303 and sigma=1.00 has median 0.10 L predator^-1 day^-1 and deliberately wide uncertainty for differences among taxa and arenas.
source: Holling, Principles of Animal Predation; Begon, Townsend & Harper, Ecology: From Individuals to Ecosystems

statement: For the same style of small predator-prey functional-response model, h_handling is the time a predator spends capturing, consuming, and digesting one prey item. A LogNormal prior with log-scale mu=-3.178 and sigma=0.70 has median about 0.0417 days per prey, or one hour per prey, with broad multiplicative variation.
source: Holling, Principles of Animal Predation; Begon, Townsend & Harper, Ecology: From Individuals to Ecosystems

statement: In consumer-resource or Lotka-Volterra-type predator-prey models, e_conversion is the fraction of consumed prey biomass converted into predator biomass or offspring. A Beta(3,7) prior has mean 0.30 and supports the common ecological expectation that conversion efficiency is positive but well below one.
source: Yodzis & Innes body-size consumer-resource model; Sibly, Brown & Kodric-Brown, Metabolic Ecology

statement: For a single-species growth model with a strong Allee effect, A_over_K is the critical abundance threshold expressed as a fraction of carrying capacity. A Beta(2,8) prior has mean 0.20 and keeps most mass below one-half of K, appropriate when the threshold is believed to occur at relatively low density but is poorly known.
source: Courchamp, Berec & Gascoigne, Allee Effects in Ecology and Conservation
